question: Explain the mechanism by which cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4 (CTLA-4) and programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1) immune checkpoint pathways inhibit T-cell activation and how this suppression is therapeutically exploited in cancer immunotherapy.
answer:
CTLA-4 and PD-1 are negative costimulatory receptors that enforce immune homeostasis by dampening T-cell activation. During antigen presentation, T-cell receptor (TCR) engagement with cognate peptide–MHC complexes on dendritic cells (DCs) triggers a primary activation signal. Efficient full activation, however, requires a second costimulatory signal mediated by CD28 binding to CD80/CD86 on antigen-presenting cells (APCs). CTLA-4 is upregulated on activated T cells within 24–48 hours and binds CD80/CD86 with ~20–50-fold higher affinity than CD28, thereby outcompeting the positive signal and arresting T-cell proliferation. Intracellularly, CTLA-4 recruits phosphatases SHP-2 and PP2A to the immunological synapse, dephosphorylating proximal TCR-proximal signaling proteins such as LCK, ZAP-70, and CD3ζ, resulting in calcium flux reduction and decreased IL-2 transcription.

PD-1 is inducibly expressed on activated T cells, NK cells, and B cells upon exposure to IFN-γ or chronic antigen exposure, typical of the tumor microenvironment. Its ligands PD-L1 and PD-L2 are variably expressed on tumor cells, stromal cells, and APCs. PD-1 ligation recruits SHP-1 and SHP-2 to its cytoplasmic immunoreceptor tyrosine-based inhibitory motif (ITIM) and immunoreceptor tyrosine-based switch motif (ITSM), leading to dephosphorylation of CD28, PI3K, and Akt, thereby attenuating mTOR signaling and glucose metabolism essential for effector function. PD-1 also reduces T-cell motility and integrin activation, limiting migration into tumor nests.

Therapeutically, monoclonal antibodies targeting CTLA-4 (ipilimumab) and PD-1/PD-L1 (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, atezolizumab) disrupt these inhibitory interactions. By blocking CTLA-4, ipilimumab enhances dendritic-cell mediated priming in lymph nodes; by blocking PD-1/PD-L1, checkpoint inhibitors restore cytotoxic function and metabolic fitness of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. Recent phase III trials such as CheckMate-067 (nivolumab + ipilimumab) demonstrated a 5-year overall survival of 44% in advanced melanoma, underscoring synergistic efficacy when both pathways are modulated. Newer agents targeting alternative checkpoints (e.g., TIGIT, LAG-3) are being evaluated in combination to overcome resistance.

Summary: The CTLA-4 and PD-1 pathways inhibit T-cell activation through high-affinity ligand competition and inhibitory phosphatase recruitment, respectively. Therapeutic blockade reverses tumor-induced anergy, enhances anti-tumor immunity, and has revolutionized oncology, with ongoing research focused on biomarker-guided combinations to improve response durability.
